title: Prepare for the Unexpected
summary: A principles-focused speech at a farewell symposium for Klaas Knot, laying out three pillars of successful monetary policy—accountability and independence, transparency, and well-anchored inflation expectations—and arguing central banks must 'prepare for the unexpected' with robust strategies and a broad toolkit beyond the short-term rate. It is conceptual and offers essentially no read on the current U.S. stance, inflation outlook, or near-term policy path. The most policy-relevant content is its defense of central-bank independence and its emphasis that anchored expectations short-circuit second-round effects. As a framework speech it carries no clear hawkish or dovish lean.

key_quotes.quote: Central banks must own the responsibility to deliver price stability and have the independence of action and the ability to achieve it.
key_quotes.context: First of three principles—accountability and central-bank independence

key_quotes.quote: Well-anchored inflation expectations short-circuit so-called second-round effects in wage and price setting that exacerbate and prolong the impacts of the kinds of shocks we saw during the 1970s.
key_quotes.context: On why anchored expectations matter for controlling inflation

key_quotes.quote: It’s important to be nimble in execution even as one is steady in strategy.
key_quotes.context: Summarizing the robust-policy approach to uncertainty
